issues.description: The inventory endpoints (PATCH and GET) have no authentication guard. The decorator `@x-autobe-authorization-actor null` confirms no auth is applied. The spec comments mention seller authentication and ownership checks, but the controller methods accept no auth parameter and pass no actor to the providers. Any unauthenticated user can query inventory records for any variant.
issues.suggestion: Add a SellerAuth (or appropriate role) decorator to both `index` and `at` methods, inject the seller payload, and pass it to the provider functions so ownership can be enforced.

issues.description: All controllers use `console.log(error)` for error handling. This is the standard AutoBE pattern but may leak stack traces or sensitive data to logs without structured context.
issues.suggestion: Consider using a structured logger (e.g., NestJS Logger) with log levels and sanitized error messages across all controllers.

issues.description: Rate limiting is not applied to any endpoint in this codebase. High-frequency requests to inventory or analytics endpoints could be used for data harvesting.
issues.suggestion: Apply rate limiting at the API gateway or using NestJS ThrottlerModule, especially on sensitive data endpoints.

issues.description: When a promotion request is approved, a new admin account is created with a hardcoded placeholder password hash ('PLACEHOLDER'). This creates an admin account with no real credentials, which could be exploited if an attacker can trigger a login with a known-bad hash or if the system has any fallback authentication logic.
issues.suggestion: Do not create admin accounts with placeholder passwords. Either require the requester to set a password as part of the approval flow, send a password-reset email, or generate a secure random temporary password using bcrypt/argon2 before storing.

issues.description: This provider lists admin promotion requests and is scoped to a CustomerPayload, but the where clause does not filter by the authenticated customer's ID. Any authenticated customer can query all promotion requests across all customers, including other users' promotion request data.
issues.suggestion: Add a filter condition to restrict results to the authenticated customer: add `customer_id: props.customer.id` (or equivalent join condition) to the whereInput so customers can only see their own promotion requests.

issues.description: The function fetches the image to verify it belongs to the product but never actually performs the soft-delete operation. The image record is fetched but no UPDATE or DELETE statement is executed, meaning the endpoint silently succeeds without deleting anything.
issues.suggestion: After verifying ownership, add the actual delete operation: `await MyGlobal.prisma.ecommerce_mall_product_images.update({ where: { id: props.imageId }, data: { deleted_at: new Date() } });`

issues.description: The authorization check uses a loose OR condition: `request.seller?.id === props.seller.id || request.orderItem.product.seller.id === props.seller.id`. If `request.seller` is null/undefined, the first condition is false and the second is evaluated. This could allow a seller who owns the product (but not the cancellation request's assigned seller) to access cancellation requests they shouldn't see. Additionally, if `request.orderItem.product.seller` is undefined, this will throw a runtime error rather than returning 403.
issues.suggestion: Use a single, explicit ownership check tied to the cancellation request's seller_id field. Ensure null-safety with optional chaining and default to denying access if any part of the chain is undefined.

issues.description: The provider fetches ALL variants from the database a second time (without pagination) to compute an accurate filtered count. For products with large numbers of variants, this causes a full table scan and loads all records into memory, creating a potential DoS vector through memory exhaustion and slow queries.
issues.suggestion: Move filtering logic into the Prisma where clause so the database handles filtering. Use a single COUNT query with the same filter conditions instead of fetching all records into application memory.

issues.description: The refresh token flow does not invalidate the old refresh token after issuing a new one. This means a stolen refresh token remains valid indefinitely until session expiry, enabling token replay attacks. Additionally, the session expiry is extended on every refresh without a maximum lifetime cap.
issues.suggestion: Implement refresh token rotation: invalidate the old refresh token (e.g., store a token version or hash in the session record) when issuing a new one. Add a maximum absolute session lifetime to prevent indefinite extension.

issues.description: The function uses `typia.random<string & tags.Format<'uuid'>>()` to generate random UUIDs for image summary objects in the comparison response. These IDs are fabricated and do not correspond to actual database records, which could mislead API consumers into using these fake IDs for subsequent operations.
issues.suggestion: Use the actual image IDs from the database records (`afterImg.id`, `beforeImg.id`) instead of generating random UUIDs. The real IDs are already available in the fetched snapshot data.

issues.description: The audit log search endpoint defaults to a limit of 100 records per page with no enforced maximum. Large page sizes could cause performance issues or expose excessive data in a single response.
issues.suggestion: Enforce a maximum page size (e.g., 100) and validate that the requested limit does not exceed it.

issues.description: Access and refresh tokens are both signed with the same JWT_SECRET_KEY and there is no differentiation enforced at the signing level between access and refresh tokens (only a 'tokenType' claim in the payload). A stolen access token cannot be used as a refresh token due to the claim check, but using a single secret for both token types reduces the security boundary.
issues.suggestion: Use separate secrets for access tokens and refresh tokens (e.g., JWT_ACCESS_SECRET and JWT_REFRESH_SECRET), or use asymmetric keys. This ensures that a compromise of one secret does not affect the other token type.

issues.description: The refresh token rotation pattern is not implemented. When a new refresh token is issued, the old refresh token remains valid until its expiry. If a refresh token is stolen, both the attacker and the legitimate user can use it to obtain new access tokens until the session expires.
issues.suggestion: Implement refresh token rotation: invalidate the old refresh token (e.g., by storing a token version or hash in the session record) when issuing a new one. Detect reuse of old refresh tokens as a potential compromise signal.

issues.description: The status transition validation uses a plain object with string keys and no exhaustive check. The 'cancelled' and 'refunded' statuses are not included as source states in validTransitions, meaning items already in those terminal states could potentially be transitioned if the logic falls through to an empty array. While the current logic returns an empty array for unknown states (preventing transitions), the design is fragile.
issues.suggestion: Use an exhaustive enum or a more explicit state machine. Explicitly define all terminal states and reject any transition attempt from them with a clear error message.

issues.description: Failed refresh token attempts (invalid token, expired session, deleted account) are not logged. For super admin authentication, failed attempts should be audited.
issues.suggestion: Add structured audit logging for all failed super admin authentication attempts, including the reason for failure and any available context (IP address if accessible).

issues.description: No upper bound is enforced on the 'limit' pagination parameter. A client could request an arbitrarily large number of records in a single query, potentially causing performance issues or data over-exposure.
issues.suggestion: Cap the limit to a reasonable maximum (e.g., 100) and validate that page and limit are positive integers.

issues.description: No rate limiting is applied to product creation. A seller could create a large number of products rapidly, potentially causing storage or performance issues.
issues.suggestion: Apply rate limiting at the API gateway or NestJS middleware level for product creation endpoints.

agentEvaluations.summary: [sampled 30/382 files] [3 chunks, 18→17 issues] The most significant issue is the completely unprotected inventory endpoints in EcommercemallVariantsInventoryController, which expose inventory records and history to unauthenticated users despite the spec requiring seller authentication and ownership checks. All other controllers correctly use role-specific auth decorators (CustomerAuth, AdminAuth, SuperadminAuth, SellerAuth). The remaining findings are AutoBE-standard patterns (console.log, no rate limiting) flagged at suggestion level only.

issues.description: Using `new Date().toISOString() as unknown as Date` to assign to a Date field. `toISOString()` returns a string, not a Date object. This will cause a type mismatch at runtime.
issues.suggestion: Use `new Date()` directly instead of converting to ISO string and casting.

issues.description: The `satisfies` clause references `Prisma.ecommerce_mall_admin_promotion_request_customersFindManyArgs` and `Prisma.ecommerce_mall_admin_promotion_request_sellersFindManyArgs`. These Prisma types likely do not exist; the correct types should be based on the actual model names (e.g., `ecommerce_mall_admin_promotion_request_customer`).
issues.suggestion: Verify the actual Prisma model names and use the correct type names (e.g., `Prisma.ecommerce_mall_admin_promotion_request_customerFindManyArgs`).

issues.description: Assigning a string (`new Date().toISOString()`) to `updated_at` which is expected to be a Date field. This will cause a type mismatch.
issues.suggestion: Use `new Date()` instead of `new Date().toISOString()`.

issues.description: Using `toISOStringSafe(new Date())` to assign to `updated_at` which is a Date field. `toISOStringSafe` returns a string, not a Date.
issues.suggestion: Use `new Date()` directly.

issues.description: Function is marked as 'Cannot implement' and returns `typia.random<IEcommerceMallCustomer>()`. This is a stub that does not perform any actual update operation.
issues.suggestion: Implement the actual profile update logic or remove the endpoint if not supported.

issues.description: The transaction creates an admin record but does not check if an admin with the same email already exists, potentially creating duplicate admin accounts.
issues.suggestion: Add a uniqueness check before creating the admin record.

issues.description: When updating option values, the code deletes all existing options and creates new ones. This could cause issues with referential integrity (e.g., foreign keys in other tables) and loses historical data.
issues.suggestion: Consider updating existing options in place or using a soft delete approach.

issues.description: The response object `IEcommerceMallSeller` is constructed using snapshot data, but the interface likely requires more fields (e.g., `id`, `email`). This may not match the expected DTO structure.
issues.suggestion: Ensure the response matches the full `IEcommerceMallSeller` interface, possibly by fetching the complete seller record.
